text: In this contribution I make use of the genre of the academic letter and reflect on the period when my predecessor Prof. Johan Adam Heyns was appointed as senior lecturer at our Faculty of Theology here at Stellenbosch University from 1966 to 1970. I intend to reappraise this phase of his academic career. During that period he could concentrate extensively on apologetics and to a smaller degree on ethics. I will reflect on his views about this period and will in the process throw a sixfold mirror on this phase of his life. One of the sides of this mirror will illuminate the first struggles in his academic career between orthodoxy and modernity; between the philosophical school of thought of his supervisor Henk Stoker and the predominantly Reformed theological thinking of his academic hero Herman Bavinck, while the other side will focus on the issue of apartheid and the resistance to it. Through the lens of this mirror I intend to shed light retrospectively on the various phases of an academic career as such, including my own, while looking back reflectively on the beauty and the danger of academic freshness during young years as well as the merits and downsides of the wisdom that develops with years. (The rest of the abstract is not available. Please contact us.) Intradisciplinary and/or interdisciplinary implications: The research is not interdisciplinary in nature. This article contributes to a growing body of literature about assessing the legacy of Prof. Johan Adam Heyns.
headline: Fully Human Written
